coredata.dc:title: Room-Temperature Aluminum-Sulfur Batteries with a Lithium-Ion-Mediated Ionic Liquid Electrolyte
coredata.dc:creator: Yu, Xingwen
Boyer, Mathew J.
Hwang, Gyeong S.
Manthiram, Arumugam
coredata.dc:description: Aluminum-sulfur (Al-S) chemistry is attractive for the development of future-generation electrochemical energy storage technologies. However, to date, only limited reversible Al-S chemistry has been demonstrated. This paper demonstrates a highly reversible room-temperature Al-S battery with a lithium-ion (Li+-ion)-mediated ionic liquid electrolyte. Mechanistic studies with electrochemical and spectroscopic methodologies revealed that the enhancement in reversibility by Li+-ion mediation is attributed to the chemical reactivation of aluminum polysulfides and/or sulfide by Li+ during electrochemical cycling. The results obtained with X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy and density functional theory calculations suggest the presence of a Li3AlS3-like product with a mixture of Li2S- and Al2S3-like phases in the discharged sulfur cathode. With Li+-ion mediation, the cycle life of room-temperature Al-S batteries is greatly improved. The cell delivers an initial capacity of ∼1,000 mA hr g−1 and maintains a capacity of up to 600 mA hr g−1 after 50 cycles.
coredata.dcterms:subject: SDG7: Affordable and clean energy
aluminum-sulfur battery
ionic liquid electrolyte
Li+-ion mediation
electrochemical mechanism
density functional theory calculation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Battery Performance Testing and Electrochemical Experiments

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This article is made available under the Elsevier license.

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: US Department of Energy Office of Science

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Materials Sciences and Engineering Division of the US Department of Energy Office of Science under award number DE-SC0005397 . G.S.H. also greatly acknowledges the Robert A. Welch Foundation ( F-1535 ) for partial financial support of the computational work and the Texas Advanced Computing Center for providing high-performance computing resources. The authors thank Mr. Sanjay Nanda and Ms. Haley Stowe for their assistance with the XPS data collection and the quantum chemical calculations, respectively.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Electrochemical Characteristics and Performances of Aluminum-Sulfur Cells
(A) First and second discharge profiles of an Al ∥ Li+-Al[EMI]Cl4 ∥ S cell and an Al ∥ Al[EMI]Cl4 ∥ S cell. IL, ionic liquid.
(B) The first-cycle discharge-charge profiles of Al ∥ Li+-Al[EMI]Cl4 ∥ S cells at different discharge-charge rates.
(C) Discharge capacities and Coulombic efficiencies as a function of cycle number for the Al ∥ Li+-Al[EMI]Cl4 ∥ S cell and the Al ∥ Al[EMI]Cl4 ∥ S cell.
(D) Cyclic voltammograms of the Al ∥ Li+-Al[EMI]Cl4 ∥ S cell at a scan rate of 0.03 mV s−1.

UV-vis spectra of the sulfur cathodes discharged to a depth of 350 mA hr g−1 in the (A) Li ∥ LiCF3SO3(DME/DOL) ∥ S cell, (B) Al ∥ Al[EMI]Cl4 ∥ S cell, and (C) Al ∥ Li+-Al[EMI]Cl4 ∥ S cell. IL, ionic liquid.
X-Ray Photoelectron Spectra of Sulfur Cathodes
XPS S 2p spectra and curve fittings of the deep-discharged sulfur electrodes in the (A) Li ∥ LiCF3SO3(DME/DOL) ∥ S cell, (B) Al ∥ Al[EMI]Cl4 ∥ S cell, and (C) Al ∥ Li+-Al[EMI]Cl4 ∥ S cell.
Radial Distribution Functions for Al-S, S-Li, and S-S Pairs as Predicted from AIMD Simulations

The inset shows an AIMD snapshot with two representative Al-S clusters. Blue, yellow, and pink balls represent Al, S, and Li atoms, respectively. Each system contains 24 Al, 72 S, and 72 Li atoms in a cubic periodic box with an edge length of around 14 Å, corresponding to Li3AlS3 with a density of 2.06 ± 0.03 g cm−3.
Atomic Percentages of Sulfur Corresponding to the Li2S- and Al2S3-like Features in the Discharged Sulfur Cathode of the Al ∥ Li+-Al[EMI]Cl4 ∥ S Cell

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Data were obtained from fitting the XPS curves in Figure 3.

head.ce:title: Room-Temperature Aluminum-Sulfur Batteries with a Lithium-Ion-Mediated Ionic Liquid Electrolyte

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Materials Science & Engineering Program and Texas Materials Institute, The University of Texas at Austin, Austin, TX 78712, USA

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Materials Science & Engineering Program and Texas Materials Institute
The University of Texas at Austin

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, The University of Texas at Austin, Austin, TX 78712, USA

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The University of Texas at Austin

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Aluminum-sulfur (Al-S) chemistry is attractive for the development of future-generation electrochemical energy storage technologies. However, to date, only limited reversible Al-S chemistry has been demonstrated. This paper demonstrates a highly reversible room-temperature Al-S battery with a lithium-ion (Li+-ion)-mediated ionic liquid electrolyte. Mechanistic studies with electrochemical and spectroscopic methodologies revealed that the enhancement in reversibility by Li+-ion mediation is attributed to the chemical reactivation of aluminum polysulfides and/or sulfide by Li+ during electrochemical cycling. The results obtained with X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy and density functional theory calculations suggest the presence of a Li3AlS3-like product with a mixture of Li2S- and Al2S3-like phases in the discharged sulfur cathode. With Li+-ion mediation, the cycle life of room-temperature Al-S batteries is greatly improved. The cell delivers an initial capacity of ∼1,000 mA hr g−1 and maintains a capacity of up to 600 mA hr g−1 after 50 cycles.

Batteries represent an indispensable technology for electrifying the transportation sector and for storing electricity produced from renewable sources. Ambient-temperature non-aqueous sulfur battery chemistries are becoming increasingly appealing for these applications. Although lithium-sulfur batteries have been extensively investigated in recent years, aluminum-sulfur (Al-S) chemistry is more attractive from an economical, sustainability, and safety point of view. However, because of a lack of effective electrolytes, only limited reversibility has been realized so far with Al-S chemistry.

This paper presents a lithium-ion mediation strategy for enhancing the reversibility of Al-S chemistry at ambient temperatures. A lithium-ion-mediated ionic liquid electrolyte remarkably improves the electrochemical performance of Al-S batteries. Based on a series of experimental and computational simulation analyses, the relevant mechanisms are presented.

Manthiram and colleagues have validated a Li+-ion mediation approach to enhance the electrochemical reversibility of ambient-temperature aluminum-sulfur (Al-S) battery chemistry. They systematically investigated the relevant mechanisms with combined experimental and theoretical methodologies. Al-S chemistry is attractive for the development of high-energy, low-cost, safe, next-generation electrochemical energy storage technologies.

ce:section.ce:para:
Non-aqueous room-temperature reversible aluminum-ion (Al-ion) batteries have recently gaining increasing attention. 1–3 The relevant research has been triggered by the feasibility of efficiently depositing and stripping aluminum in ionic liquid electrolytes at ambient temperatures. 4–8 There have been increasing studies on reversible Al-ion batteries with different types of cathode materials, including metal oxides (Mn2O4, 9 V2O5, 10–14 VO2, 15 and TiO2
16 ), metal sulfides, 17–19 conductive polymers, 20 various carbon-based materials, 21–24 and LiFePO4. 25 The Al-ion batteries developed with the above cathodes, ionic liquid electrolytes, and an Al-metal anode have demonstrated remarkable cycling performance. However, previous research has also recognized the challenges of developing Al-ion batteries with insertion-type cathodes. One major concern is the high charge density of the trivalent Al3+ ions. Another significant issue is that the ionic aluminum species exists as a large-size chloroaluminate (Al x Cl y
−) species in ionic liquids. These two features make the intercalation reactions involving Al extremely challenging because of electrostatic repulsions and diffusional limitations.

The development of Al-ion batteries with a conversion-type cathode provides a promising alternative strategy for overcoming the challenges of intercalation reactions. Recently, non-aqueous sulfur cathode chemistry has received overwhelming attention for the development of alkali-metal/sulfur and alkaline-earth-metal/sulfur batteries, such as Li-S batteries, 26–30 Na-S batteries, 31–35 K-S batteries, 36 Mg-S batteries, 37–41 and Ca-S batteries. 42 Sulfur can deliver an extremely high theoretical capacity of 1,672 mA hr g−1, which is about ten times higher than that of traditional metal oxide cathodes. Sulfur is also an abundant element with rich resources and is inexpensive. Although the Li-S system has mostly been emphasized over the past few years, from an energy-cost point of view, Al would be a more attractive option than Li for coupling with a sulfur cathode because of the high abundance and the low cost of Al. The theoretical voltages of Li-S and Al-S couples are ca. 2.63 and 1.27 V, respectively. However, because of the extremely low cost of Al, the energy cost of the Al-S battery is only about one-tenth of that of Li-S batteries. 43 Therefore, coupling an earth-abundant aluminum anode with a sulfur cathode would lead to an aluminum-sulfur (Al-S) battery that could offer a high-energy-density, low-cost, and safe energy storage system.

So far, there have been only three research reports on ambient-temperature Al-S batteries. 43–45 Chloroaluminate ionic liquid electrolyte can enable the reversibility of Al-S chemistry at ambient temperature. However, Al-S cells exhibited poor electrochemical reversibility and suffered from sluggish reaction kinetics. Therefore, the cells displayed a short cycle life (only 20 reversible charge-discharge cycles). Here, we present a lithium-ion (Li+-ion) mediation strategy for enhancing the cyclability of Al-S batteries. The Al-S cells are operated with a Li+-ion-mediated Al ionic liquid electrolyte. The reversibility of Al-S batteries is greatly improved with this mediator-ion approach. The cells deliver an initial capacity of ∼1,000 mAh g−1 and maintain a capacity of up to 600 mA hr g−1 after 50 cycles. Possible mechanisms for enhancement of the reversibility are proposed on the basis of the electrochemical and spectroscopic data as well as density functional theory (DFT) calculations.

The Li+-ion mediated ionic liquid electrolytes were prepared as described in the Experimental Procedures. We generally synthesized them by mixing solid aluminum chloride (AlCl3) and solid 1-ethyl-3-methylimidazolium chloride ([EMI]Cl) in an argon-filled glove box to render an ionic liquid. Then, lithium salt was dissolved into the above ionic liquid. The resulting electrolyte is termed Li+-Al[EMI]Cl4 (the ionic liquid without lithium salt is termed Al[EMI]Cl4). Al-S cells were fabricated with a cathode-separator assembly developed in house as described in our previous publication 43 and as schematized in Figure S1. A piece of pre-activated, self-woven carbon nanofiber (CNF) paper was used as the cathode matrix on which the active sulfur powder was dispersed. A thin layer of single-wall carbon nanotube (SWCNT) was coated onto a piece of glass-fiber (GF) separator (as pictured in Figure S2). The active sulfur was sandwiched between the SWCNT coating and the CNF matrix in the cathode-separator assembly. The anode of the cell was a piece of aluminum foil. The Al-S cell configuration in this study with the above described cathode-separator assembly is schematized in Figure S3. Cells with Li+-ion-mediated ionic liquid electrolyte are termed Al ∥ Li+-Al[EMI]Cl4 ∥ S cells. Accordingly, cells prepared with the regular ionic liquid electrolyte without Li+-ion salt are termed Al ∥ Al[EMI]Cl4 ∥ S cells.

Figure 1 summarizes the electrochemical performance of Al-S cells prepared with Li+-ion-mediated ionic liquid electrolyte and regular ionic liquid electrolyte. The first- and second-cycle discharge profiles of the two Al-S cells (at the same cycling rate of C/50) are compared in Figure 1A. The initial discharge capacities delivered by the two cells are quite similar. However, the shape of the discharge curves is different. The discharge curve of the Al ∥ Al[EMI]Cl4 ∥ S cell exhibits a small plateau at a relatively high voltage (0.9–1.2 V), followed by a long flat plateau at a relatively low voltage (as shown in Figure S4), whereas the discharge curve of the Al ∥ Li+-Al[EMI]Cl4 ∥ S cell shows a relatively longer high-voltage region. Specific discharge capacities in the high-voltage region are ∼180 mA hr g−1 for the Al ∥ Al[EMI]Cl4 ∥ S cell and ∼290 mA hr g−1 for the Al ∥ Li+-Al[EMI]Cl4 ∥ S cell. After these values are calibrated in consideration of the overall unitization of the sulfur cathode (∼75%, 1,250 mA hr g−1 practical capacity versus 1,672 mA hr g−1 theoretical capacity), the high-voltage regions correspond to theoretical discharge capacities of ∼250 mA hr g−1 for the Al ∥ Al[EMI]Cl4 ∥ S cell and ∼390 mA hr g−1 for the Al ∥ Li+-Al[EMI]Cl4 ∥ S cell. When these values are compared with the theoretical capacity of forming different polysulfide species (as provided in Table S1), the intermediate discharge products of the high-voltage regions are supposed to be S6 2− (average stoichiometry) species (250 mA hr g−1 practical capacity versus 279 mA hr g−1 theoretical capacity) for the Al ∥ Al[EMI]Cl4 ∥ S cell and S4 2− (average stoichiometry) species (390 mA hr g−1 practical capacity versus 419 mA hr g−1 theoretical capacity) for the Al ∥ Li+-Al[EMI]Cl4 ∥ S cell. According to a recently proposed discharge mechanism for Al-S batteries, 43 as well as those for other metal-sulfur batteries, 40,46–49 the high-voltage discharge region (either exhibiting a slope or a plateau) is generally attributed to the conversion of elemental sulfur to soluble high-order polysulfide species. Therefore, the electrochemical characteristics shown in Figure 1A imply that the Li+ ion improves the solubility of polysulfide species. We also investigated the Li+-ion mediation effect with another lithium salt, lithium bis(trifluoromethanesulfonyl)imide (LiTFSI). No obvious difference was observed in the discharge curves between the cells prepared with the LiCF3SO3-mediated and the LiTFSI-mediated ionic liquids, as compared in Figure S5. In the second cycle, the high-voltage plateau of the Al ∥ Al[EMI]Cl4 ∥ S cell disappeared (Figures 1A and S4). This phenomenon has previously been explained as resulting from the poor reversibility between elemental sulfur and high-order polysulfides (S6 2−). 43 However, for the Al ∥ Li+-Al[EMI]Cl4 ∥ S cell, there was no significant difference in the discharge curves between the first and the second cycles (Figures 1A and S4).

Figure 1B displays the first-cycle discharge-charge profiles of the Al ∥ Li+-Al[EMI]Cl4 ∥ S cell at different cycling rates. As seen in Figure 1B, a significant rising-point voltage does not occur in the charge profile of the Al ∥ Li+-Al[EMI]Cl4 ∥ S cells. This phenomenon may be a result of polysulfide shuttling. Therefore, during discharge, we set a cutoff voltage of 0.45 V at each C rate and strategically charged the cell to a capacity of 106% of its discharge capacity. In each case, the discharge curve exhibits a high-voltage discharge region, followed by a long discharge plateau at a relatively low voltage. Discharge capacities of the Al ∥ Li+-Al[EMI]Cl4 ∥ S cells at C/50, C/20, and C/10 rates are ∼1,250, ∼1,000, and ∼860 mA hr g−1, respectively, and the main discharge plateau voltages are ∼0.76, ∼0.72, and ∼0.65 V, respectively. The voltage hysteresis between the discharge and charge profiles is most likely a result of the relatively low conductivity of the ionic liquid electrolyte and the kinetic barrier of the electrode reactions.

Figure 1C shows the long-term cycling performances of the Al ∥ Li+-Al[EMI]Cl4 ∥ S cell and the Al ∥ Al[EMI]Cl4 ∥ S cell in terms of their discharge capacities and Coulombic efficiencies as a function of cycle number. As already presented in Figure 1A, the Al ∥ Li+-Al[EMI]Cl4 ∥ S cell and the Al ∥ Al[EMI]Cl4 ∥ S cell delivered similar initial discharge capacities in the first cycle. After that, the discharge capacity of the Al ∥ Al[EMI]Cl4 ∥ S cell declined continuously at a relatively fast rate. At the tenth cycle, the discharge capacity of the Al ∥ Al[EMI]Cl4 ∥ S cell remained only at 550 mAh g−1. Then, the capacity fade of this cell became even faster. As seen in Figure 1C, the Al ∥ Li+-Al[EMI]Cl4 ∥ S cell also exhibited continuous capacity degradation with prolonged cycling, but the capacity fade rate was much lower than that of the Al ∥ Al[EMI]Cl4 ∥ S cell. With the Li+-ion-mediated ionic electrolyte, the cycle life of the Al ∥ Li+-Al[EMI]Cl4 ∥ S cell was greatly improved. In addition, the Al ∥ Li+-Al[EMI]Cl4 ∥ S cells showed relatively higher Coulombic efficiencies than the Al ∥ Al[EMI]Cl4 ∥ S cell. The discharge capacity remained at ∼ 600 mA hr g−1 after 50 cycles. Figure S6 compares the charge-discharge profiles (at a C/20 rate) of the Al ∥ Li+-Al[EMI]Cl4 ∥ S cell at the first and 50th cycles. In addition to the capacity decline, the Al ∥ Li+-Al[EMI]Cl4 ∥ S cell showed increased voltage polarization after 50 cycles, most likely as a result of the worse interfacial property of the cycled cell components.

Figure 1D presents the cyclic voltammograms (CVs) of the Al ∥ Li+-Al[EMI]Cl4 ∥ S cell. We performed the CV experiments by initially scanning the cell from the open-circuit voltage (OCV ∼ 1.30 V) to 0.25 V in the negative direction. Then, eight full potentiostatic cycles were executed with the cell between 0.25 and 2.10 V. With prolonged cycling, declines in both the charge (oxidation) and the discharge (reduction) peaks in the CVs could be observed, which is in agreement with the continuous capacity decline of the Al ∥ Li+-Al[EMI]Cl4 ∥ S cell, as presented in Figure 1C. All the cathodic (reduction) curves show a very small current wave around ∼1.10 V and a relatively large current wave at ∼ 0.65 V. The first small current peak corresponds to the high-voltage discharge region of the discharge profile of the Al ∥ Li+-Al[EMI]Cl4 ∥ S cell in Figure 1A. The large second reduction peak corresponds to the major discharge plateau of Figure 1A at relatively low voltage. However, for the Al ∥ Al[EMI]Cl4 ∥ S cell, only a high-voltage cathodic peak appeared in the first CV cycle (as shown in Figure S7). After the second cycle, the CVs of the Al ∥ Al[EMI]Cl4 ∥ S cell displayed only a single reduction peak. This feature is consistent with the result that the high-voltage discharge plateau disappeared after the first cycle for the Al ∥ Al[EMI]Cl4 ∥ S cell (Figures 1A and S4).

In order to gain a mechanistic insight into the Li+-ion mediation effects, we performed a sequence of spectroscopic analyses. The discharge intermediate products of the Al ∥ Li+-Al[EMI]Cl4 ∥ S cell and the Al ∥ Al[EMI]Cl4 ∥ S cell were investigated with UV-visible (UV-vis) absorption spectroscopy. For comparison, the UV-vis experiment was also performed for a Li-S cell that was prepared with a Li-metal anode and a typical Li-S battery electrolyte (LiCF3SO3 dissolved in a mixture of dimethoxyethane and 1,3-dioxolane [DME/DOL]). The resulting Li-S cell is termed a Li ∥ LiCF3SO3(DME/DOL) ∥ S cell. Before the UV-vis samples were collected, all cells were pre-discharged to an assigned depth with a specific cathode capacity of 350 mA hr g−1 sulfur. According to the calculation results shown in Table S1, the discharge products delivered at this discharge depth are expected to be S6 2− and S4 2−. We collected the liquid samples by soaking the cathode-separator assemblies taken from the discharged cells into the Al[EMI]Cl4 ionic liquid for the Al ∥ Al[EMI]Cl4 ∥ S cell, into the Li+-Al[EMI]Cl4 ionic liquid for the Al ∥ Li+-Al[EMI]Cl4 ∥ S cell, or into the DME/DOL solvent for the Li-S cell for 48 hr. Figure 2 compares the UV-vis spectra of the discharged sulfur cathodes from the Li ∥ LiCF3SO3(DME/DOL) ∥ S cell, Al ∥ Li+-Al[EMI]Cl4 ∥ S cell, and Al ∥ Al[EMI]Cl4 ∥ S cell. Two major absorbance peaks at 330 and 430 nm can be observed for the samples from the discharged Al ∥ Li+-Al[EMI]Cl4 ∥ S cell, indicating the presence of soluble S6 2− and S4 2− species. 50–54 This implies that the elemental sulfur dissociated into soluble S6 2− and S4 2− species as expected at the assigned depth of discharge. However, for the Al ∥ Al[EMI]Cl4 ∥ S cell, only one peak at 330 nm can be observed, implying the existence of a soluble species of S6 2, but the S4 2− species formed was insoluble in the ionic liquid electrolyte. The above UV-vis experimental results reveal that Li+-ion mediation improves the solubility of S4 2− species, which supports the differences in the electrochemical characteristics between the Al ∥ Li+-Al[EMI]Cl4 ∥ S cell and the Al ∥ Al[EMI]Cl4 ∥ S cell (compared in Figures 1A and S4).

During discharge, the utilization of active sulfur is ca. 60% if we compare the practical capacity of the sulfur cathode (1,000 mA hr g−1) with the theoretical capacity of sulfur (1,672 mA hr g−1). From the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and DFT analyses, the ratio of Li to Al and S in the final discharge product (Li3AlS3) was 3:1:3. As specified in the Experimental Procedures, the total amounts of Li+ ions and active sulfur in a coin cell are 0.06 and 0.035 mol, respectively. Therefore, upon a deep discharge, ca. 0.021 mol of sulfur and ca. 0.021 mol of Li+ ions would be consumed. The amount of Li+ ions in these studied cells was actually in excess. From the electrochemical data presented in Figure 1, the mediator Li+ ion remarkably improved the cycling performance of the Al-S batteries, but the addition of a Li+ salt to the ionic liquid electrolyte decreased the overall energy density of the Al-S cells slightly. However, the necessity of using an excess amount of Li+ ions in the cell system is not clear at present. The ratio of mediator Li+ ion to active sulfur will be optimized in the future.

To further understand the Li+-ion mediation mechanism, we analyzed the final discharge products of the Al ∥ Li+-Al[EMI]Cl4 ∥ S cell and the Al ∥ Al[EMI]Cl4 ∥ S cell with X-ray techniques. Deep-discharged sulfur cathodes (discharged to 0.2 V) were first characterized with X-ray diffraction (XRD). However, no valuable information was obtained. The XRD patterns provided in Figure S8 do not show any sulfur signals. This is possibly a result of the poorly crystalline or amorphous nature of the discharge products. Therefore, XPS analyses were performed. In order to minimize the effects from the carbon nanofiber matrix, we prepared the samples for the XPS experiments by depositing the discharge products of the sulfur cathode onto a titanium (Ti) foil matrix. To do this, we prepared two Al-S cells, Al ∥ Li+-Al[EMI]Cl4 ∥ S and Al ∥ Al[EMI]Cl4 ∥ S, with an uncoated GF separator and with a piece of Ti foil as cathode matrices (without any carbon material at the cathode). For comparison, a Li ∥ LiCF3SO3(DME/DOL) ∥ S cell was prepared with the same cell configuration. The anodes, cathodes, and electrolytes used for preparing these cells are summarized in Table S2. The cell configurations are schematized in Figure S9. After deep discharge (discharge to 0.2 V), the cells were opened, and the Ti foils with the discharge product deposited were taken out for XPS analysis.

Survey-scan XPS spectra of the discharged sulfur/Ti electrodes are presented in Figure S10. All spectra exhibit the characteristic peaks of sulfur, oxygen, and carbon (contamination). Al peaks are present in the two spectra of the Al-S cells, whereas Li peaks are displayed in the spectra of the Li-S cell and the Li+-ion-mediated Al-S cell. Al 2p spectra of the sulfur/Ti electrodes from the Al ∥ Li+-Al[EMI]Cl4 ∥ S and Al ∥ Al[EMI]Cl4 ∥ S cells are presented in Figure S11, in which no significant difference can be observed between the two samples. The Li 1s spectra of the sulfur/Ti electrodes from the Al ∥ Li+-Al[EMI]Cl4 ∥ S and the Li ∥ LiCF3SO3(DME/DOL) ∥ S cells are presented in Figure S12. Again, there is no obvious difference between the two electrodes. Figure S13 presents the survey-scan XPS spectra of a piece of fresh Al foil and a cycled (after 50 cycles) Al anode from an Al ∥ Li+-Al[EMI]Cl4 ∥ S cell. Clear differences between the two spectra can be seen, i.e., both the sulfur and the lithium peaks appear in the spectrum of the cycled Al anode (Figure S13B). However, as expected, the sulfur and lithium signals are not observed in the spectrum of the fresh Al foil (Figure S13A). The appearance of the sulfur and the lithium elements on the surface of the cycled Al anode implies that there is a polysulfide-migration process during the charge-discharge cycle of the Al ∥ Li+-Al[EMI]Cl4 ∥ S cell. Such a polysulfide-shuttle behavior is one of the most significant issues that is responsible for the performance degradation of the Al ∥ Li+-Al[EMI]Cl4 ∥ S cell.

Figure 3 displays the S 2p XPS spectra of the sulfur/Ti electrodes taken from the discharged Li ∥ LiCF3SO3(DME/DOL) ∥ S cell, Al ∥ Li+-Al[EMI]Cl4 ∥ S cell, and Al ∥ Al[EMI]Cl4 ∥ S cell. All spectra were deconvolved and fitted after background subtractions. The spectrum of the Li ∥ LiCF3SO3(DME/DOL) ∥ S cell (Figure 3A) shows typical Li2S-type sulfur characteristics. The doublet peaks located at 160.0 and 161.2 eV are assigned to the S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 signals, respectively. 55 Typical Al2S3-type sulfur characteristics are observed in the spectrum of the Al ∥ Al[EMI]Cl4 ∥ S cell (Figure 3B). 45 The S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 doublet peaks are located at 161.5 and 162.7 eV, respectively. In both cases, for the Li ∥ LiCF3SO3(DME/DOL) ∥ S cell and the Al ∥ Al[EMI]Cl4 ∥ S cell, the 2p3/2-to-S-2p1/2 peak area ratio is ∼2:1 as expected from the curve-fitting results. For the Al ∥ Li+-Al[EMI]Cl4 ∥ S cell, the spectrum in Figure 3C shows characteristics of a mixture of Li2S- and Al2S3-like structures. Curve-fitting results (as summarized in Table 1 ) suggest that the ratio of sulfur corresponding to the Li2S- and Al2S3-like features is approximately 1:1. This interesting result implies that the discharge product is most likely in a stoichiometric form of 3Li2S·Al2S3 or Li3AlS3. Although the definite structure of the final discharge product is difficult to confirm, this analysis at least suggests the presence of a mixture of Li2S- and Al2S3-like phases in the discharged sulfur cathode of the Al ∥ Li+-Al[EMI]Cl4 ∥ S cell.

We then performed first-principle calculations to further understand the formation and structure of the final discharge product. Using ab initio molecular dynamics (AIMD) simulations based on periodic DFT calculations, we first identified the structure of amorphous Li3AlS3 and evaluated its relative thermodynamic stability with respect to its phase segregation into Li2S and Al2S3, i.e., Li3AlS3 → 3/2Li2S + 1/2Al2S3; we considered the 3:1:3 ratio of Li, Al, and S atoms by assuming that the 1:1 ratio of the S XPS features in Figure 3C arises from the segregated Li2S and Al2S3 phases. The inset of Figure 4 shows a snapshot of the amorphous Li3AlS3 structure obtained by the melt-quenching method; the computational details are described in the Experimental Procedures. The density of amorphous Li3AlS3 is predicted to be 2.06 ± 0.03 g cm−3, corresponding to about 50% volume expansion with respect to α-S. Upon inspection of the structure, some interesting features are immediately apparent. As illustrated in Figure 4 (inset), the Al atoms tend to be surrounded by four or five S atoms forming isolated or somewhat extended Al-S complexes where the S atoms are either terminal (bonded to one Al atom) or bridging (bonded to two Al atoms). These anionic Al-S clusters are stabilized by Li+ cations.

We conducted a more detailed structural analysis by computing radial distribution functions (RDFs) between Al, S, and Li atoms. The RDF is given by g(r) = 〈n(r, r + dr)/4πr 2 ρdr〉, where n(r, r + dr) is the number of chosen atoms in a spherical shell of radius dr (from the reference atom), and ρ is the bulk number density. The g(r) values in Figure 4 were obtained over 5 ps AIMD trajectories across three sample systems with different atomic configurations. The g(r) for Al-S pairs shows a distinct peak at r = 2.25 Å, whereas the S-Li g(r) has a broader peak around r = 2.5 Å. Neither Al-S nor S-Li g(r) exhibits a distinct second peak, which is apparently a result of the thermal motion of the corresponding atoms, as typically seen in an amorphous structure. The first peak positions of the Al-S and S-Li g(r) are close to the Al-S distance of 2.28 Å in crystalline Al2S3 and the Li-S distance of 2.47 Å in crystalline Li2S, respectively. In addition, analysis of g(r) showed that there are no S–S bonds, indicating no polysulfides in the amorphous Li3AlS3 structure. Integrating g(r) yielded the coordination number (CN) for each reference atom. By cutting off the CN at the location of the first minimum in g(r), we could obtain the average number of surrounding atoms. From the predicted amorphous Li3AlS3 samples, Al was found to be coordinated by 4.2 S atoms on average, and S was coordinated by 1.4 Al and 4.6 Li atoms on average. A more rigorous analysis of the structure showed that all the Al atoms were 4-fold (≈80%) or 5-fold (≈20%) coordinated, forming tetrahedra and square pyramids, respectively. In addition, the ratio of terminal to bridging S atoms was found to be roughly 5:4. Each terminal S was surrounded by an average of 5.3 Li atoms, and each bridge S was surrounded by an average of 3.3 Li atoms.

Our DFT calculations predicted a positive enthalpy of mixing (ΔH mix ≈ 0.5 eV per Li3AlS3 formula unit) for the amorphous Li3AlS3 phase with respect to crystalline Li2S and Al2S3. This could suggest that the Li2S- and Al2S3-like phases would not be completely miscible, although the positive value of ΔH mix could substantially decrease, considering the possible amorphization of Li2S and Al2S3. To better understand the origins of the XPS S 2p spectra, we also calculated the S 2p binding energies by using quantum chemical calculations at the B3LYP/6-311++G(d,p) level of theory. Interestingly, the predicted S 2p binding energy of 156.8 eV for a model cluster (mimicking the Al-S clusters in amorphous Li3AlS3) is very close to 156.3 eV, as predicted for a Li2S cluster, whereas a model cluster (mimicking Al2S3) yields a noticeably higher S 2p binding energy (158.9 eV). This analysis, in addition to the absence of long-range ordering (see Figure S8), leads us to speculate that small Al2S3 crystallites may form within an amorphous LiAlS matrix; the Al2S3 crystallite size and the LiAlS composition could be determined by the relative amounts of incorporated Li and Al.

Previous studies on Al-S chemistry have indicated that the reduction of sulfur is mostly via a solid-solid transition process. 43–45 Both the intermediate and final reduction products are insoluble in ionic liquid electrolytes. The insulating nature and insolubility of low-order polysulfides pose a high kinetic barrier during electrochemical cycling of the Al-S cells. 43 In addition, breaking the Al=S double bond in Al2S3 (full discharge product) is extra challenging during charge. 43–45 In this study, as reflected from the electrochemical and UV-vis analyses, the solubility of low-order Al polysulfides increased with the assistance of Li+-ion mediation. From a mechanistic point of view, the presence of Li+ ions enables reactivation of the short-chain Al polysulfides either through an ion-exchange reaction transforming them into Al-Li polysulfides or through a strong coordination of Li+ ions with the surface of Al polysulfides to form Al-Li polysulfides, which further increases their solubility. The electrochemical kinetic barrier for the reduction or oxidation of Al polysulfides is therefore reduced. This mechanism is quite similar to that in a previous study with respect to the Li+-ion-activation effect on Mg-S chemistry. 37 The Li+ ion reactivates the low-order Mg polysulfides (e.g., MgS and MgS2) via an ion-exchange reaction and converts the low-order Mg polysulfides (MgS and MgS2) into relatively more reversible low-order Li polysulfides (Li2S and Li2S2). 37 The reactivation process could also be via a strong coordination of Li+ ions with the surface S2− and S2 2− of the low-order Mg polysulfides (MgS and MgS2), which can increase the solubility of low-order Mg polysulfides and reduce the electrochemical kinetics barrier through the formation of MgLi-PS species. 37 Furthermore, as reflected in the XPS analyses and the DFT calculations in this study (Figures 3 and 4), the final discharge product of the Li+-ion-mediated cell involves a Li3AlS3-like species. The coordination of Li to S minimizes the formation of Al=S double bonds upon full discharge of the cell, thus promoting reactivation of sulfide species during charge.

From the cell-performance point of view, although the Li+-ion mediation approach significantly improves the reversibility of room-temperature Al-S chemistry, it is still a long way from realizing a practical battery technology. Performance degradation was still very severe during cycling of the cell. Many possibilities could contribute to the deterioration of the cell performance. In comparison with the Li-S or Na-S system, the volume change of the sulfur cathode in the Al-S cell is supposed to be much more prominent. From the anode-chemistry point of view, the discharge of Al does not form an Al3+ ion but rather forms an Al2Cl7 − species. The negatively charged transporting Al2Cl7 − species with a large ionic size could have a negative impact on the kinetics of the cell reaction. Therefore, Al-S battery chemistry, even mediated by Li+ ions, is believed to be more challenging and more complicated than its predecessor Li-S and Na-S systems. Much effort and research need to be pursued in the future to allow Al-S batteries to enter into the mainstream of non-aqueous room-temperature metal-sulfur battery technologies.

In summary, we have demonstrated a highly reversible room-temperature Al-S battery with a Li+-ion-mediated ionic liquid electrolyte. The cycle life of room-temperature Al-S batteries was significantly enhanced by the addition of Li salt (LiCF3SO3) to an Al[EMI]Cl4 ionic liquid. The electrochemical mechanism of the room-temperature Al-S battery was investigated with electrochemical and spectroscopic techniques, as well as DFT calculations. The presence of Li+ ions in the Al[EMI]Cl4 ionic liquid promoted the formation of soluble polysulfide intermediates, which lowered the electrochemical kinetic barrier for reduction or oxidation of Al polysulfides. The results obtained with XPS analyses and DFT calculations suggest that the presence of Li+ ions in the electrolyte promotes the reactivation of sulfide species through suppression of the formation of Al=S double bonds upon full discharge of the sulfur cathode. Operating with the Li+-ion-mediated ionic liquid electrolyte, Al-S batteries deliver an initial cathode capacity of ∼1,000 mA hr g−1 and maintain a capacity of up to 600 mA hr g−1 after 50 cycles.

An aluminum ionic liquid electrolyte (Al[EMI]Cl4) was prepared according to our previous synthesis method through a reaction between AlCl3 and [EMI]Cl. 43 We then prepared the ratio of AlCl3 to [EMI]Cl was 1:1.25 (mol ratio). Li+-ion-mediated electrolytes by dissolving a certain amount of lithium trifluoromethanesulfonate (LiCF3SO3) or LiTFSI into the Al[EMI]Cl4 ionic liquid. The concentration of Li salt was maintained at 0.5 M. We prepared an electrolyte for fabricating Li-S control cells by dissolving a certain amount of LiCF3SO3 salt in a binary solvent comprising a mixture (1:1, v:v) of DME and DOL. The concentration of Li salt was maintained at 1.0 M. The resulting electrolyte is termed as LiCF3SO3(DME/DOL). All electrolyte syntheses were performed in an argon-filled glove box.

The cathode matrix used in this study was a free-standing CNF paper prepared according to our previous method. 56 The thickness of the CNF paper was about 40–50 μm with an areal mass of ∼1.0 mg cm−2. Circular-shaped electrodes with a diameter of 1.2 cm were used for preparing coin cells. We fabricated the sulfur/CNF cathodes by dispersing a mixture of sulfur powder and electrolyte into the CNF paper matrix. The sulfur loading was ∼1.0 mg cm−2.

The Al-S batteries were prepared with a coin-cell configuration. The cathode-separator assembly was prepared as schematized in Figure S1. The sulfur/CNF cathode was covered with a piece of SWCNT-coated GF separator (Figure S2). The anode was a piece of a circular-shaped Al foil (Fisherbrand, 0.024 mm thick, >99% purity). The assembled Al-S cell is schematically illustrated in Figure S3. Li-S control cells were prepared with the same configuration but with a piece of Li foil as the anode and with a LiCF3SO3(DME/DOL) electrolyte. In order to avoid corrosion of the stainless steel coin-cell parts by the ionic liquid electrolyte, we applied thin Ti foil at both the cathode-cell can and the anode cap. Each coin cell was prepared with 1.13 mg of active sulfur in the cathode and 120 μL of electrolyte in the cell. Therefore, for the cell with the Li+-Al[EMI]Cl4 electrolyte, the total amounts of Li+ ion and active sulfur in a coin cell were 0.06 and 0.035 mol, respectively. The ratio of Li+ ion to sulfur was ca. 1.7:1 (mol ratio).
The model cells for preparing XPS samples were prepared with a Ti foil cathode matrix and an uncoated GF separator, as schematized in Figure S9.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Battery Performance Testing and Electrochemical Experiments
ce:section.ce:para: Cycling performances of the Al-S (as well as Li-S) batteries were assessed with a BT2000 Arbin cycler at room temperature. The specific capacity in this study was calculated on the basis of the mass of sulfur in the cathode. CV experiments were performed on a VoltaLab PGZ402 instrument with a scan rate of 0.03 mV s−1.

UV-vis experiments were conducted with a Varian Cary 5000 UV-vis near-infrared spectrophotometer. Surface analysis of the discharged sulfur cathodes was performed by XPS (Kratos Analytical Company) analysis. XRD patterns were obtained with a Philips X-ray diffractometer (CuKα radiation, step width of 0.02°, 4 s counting time per step).

AIMD simulations based on DFT were performed within the Born-Oppenheimer approximation with the Vienna Ab initio Simulation Package. 57 Generalized gradient approximation parametrized by Perdew, Berke, and Ernzerhof 58 was used for the exchange-correlation energy functional in DFT. We used the projector augmented-wave method 59,60 to describe the interaction between core and valence electrons and a plane-wave basis set with a kinetic energy cutoff of 300 eV. Given the lack of symmetry and structure in the aqueous solutions examined, we sampled the Brillouin zone by using only the gamma k point. The amorphous structures of Li3AlS3 were obtained by a melt-quenching method. We prepared each initial structure by annealing a system consisting of 24 Al, 72 Li, and 72 S atoms (randomly inserted in a cubic box with an initial density of 2.25 g cm−3) by using MD with a ReaxFF reactive force field. 61,62 The sample was then further annealed at 2,000 K for 20 ps by AIMD before being rapidly quenched to 300 K at a rate of 2 K fs−1, followed by careful volume optimization. All values reported are taken from 5 ps AIMD trajectories of the amorphous structures obtained at 300 K. S 2p binding energies were calculated by the natural-bond-orbital method 63 with the hybrid Becke 3-Lee-Yang-Parr (B3LYP) exchange-correlation functional and the 6-311++G(d,p) basis set, as implemented in the Gaussian 09 suite of programs. 64 The model clusters mimicking Al2S3 (a) and an Al-S cluster in amorphous Li3AlS3 (b) are shown in Figure S14.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Materials Sciences and Engineering Division of the US Department of Energy Office of Science under award number DE-SC0005397. G.S.H. also greatly acknowledges the Robert A. Welch Foundation (F-1535) for partial financial support of the computational work and the Texas Advanced Computing Center for providing high-performance computing resources. The authors thank Mr. Sanjay Nanda and Ms. Haley Stowe for their assistance with the XPS data collection and the quantum chemical calculations, respectively.

ce:section.ce:para: X.Y. and A.M. conceived the idea, performed the experiments, and wrote the manuscript. M.J.B. and G.S.H. carried out the computational modeling and helped with manuscript preparation.
The authors declare no competing interests.
Supplemental Information includes 14 figures and 2 tables and can be found with this article online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.chempr.2017.12.029.

ce:e-component.ce:label: Document S1. Figures S1–S14 and Tables S1 and S2
